desc: Samarkand is one of Central Asia's great Silk Road cities, celebrated for blue-tiled madrasas, Timurid monuments, and layered caravan-route history. It is strongest for architecture-first itineraries in spring and autumn, when temperatures are more manageable for long outdoor heritage circuits.

todo.name: Registan ensemble visit
todo.desc: Spend time at all three madrasas to appreciate scale, geometry, and restored mosaics.

todo.name: Shah-i-Zinda necropolis walk
todo.desc: Follow the processional lane of mausoleums known for extraordinary turquoise tile detail.

todo.name: Gur-e-Amir Mausoleum
todo.desc: Visit Timur’s resting place and learn about the dynasty that shaped the city’s golden era.

todo.name: Bibi-Khanym Mosque and Siab Bazaar
todo.desc: Pair monumental ruins with nearby produce and bread markets at the city’s daily social hub.

todo.name: Ulugh Beg Observatory
todo.desc: See remains of the 15th-century observatory tied to major advances in astronomical measurement.

todo.name: Traditional plov dinner and folk show
todo.desc: Sample Uzbek staples while enjoying regional music and dance performances.

wifi.note: Adequate in central hotels and cafés, with mobile data useful for translation, ride-hailing, and day-trip routing.

prac.fltNote: No regular Gatwick-Samarkand nonstop service; common routings connect via Istanbul, Abu Dhabi, or Tashkent. Return fares are often ~£260-£450 off-peak, ~£450-£650 in shoulder demand, and ~£650+ around peak holiday windows.
prac.visa: Uzbekistan allows visa-free entry for many nationalities including UK and EU for short stays; verify current permitted duration before departure.
alerts: Some monuments close earlier in winter; start heritage visits in the morning.

sweet: April-early June and September-October are usually best for Silk Road sightseeing in manageable temperatures.
